messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/
- dc: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#
- imdb: https://www.imdb.com/
- math: http://www.w3.org/2005/xpath-functions/math#
- ontolex: http://www.w3.org/ns/lemon/ontolex#
- owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/
- pqv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value/
- pr: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/
- prn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value-normalized/
- prov: http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#
- prv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value/
- ps: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/
- psn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value-normalized/
- psv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value/
- qb: http://purl.org/linked-data/cube#
- qfn: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/function#
- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/
- s: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/statement/
- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
- v: http://www.wikidata.org/value/
- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#
- wd: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/
- wdno: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/novalue/
- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.
who won the oscar over wolf of wall street

reasoning.content:
Okay, let's tackle this question: "who won the oscar over wolf of wall street". Hmm, first I need to understand what the user is asking. The phrasing is a bit unclear. "Won the Oscar over Wolf of Wall Street" – maybe they're asking which film won the Oscar for Best Picture in the year that The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated? Or perhaps who won a specific Oscar category related to The Wolf of Wall Street?

Wait, The Wolf of Wall Street (2013) was nominated for several Oscars but didn't win any major awards. Let me recall: at the 2014 Academy Awards (for 2013 films), 12 Years a Slave won Best Picture. The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated for Best Actor (Leonardo DiCaprio), Best Adapted Screenplay, and Best Supporting Actor (Jonah Hill), but didn't win. So maybe the user is confused and thinks that The Wolf of Wall Street won an Oscar, but actually, it didn't. Or maybe they're asking which film won Best Picture that year instead?

Alternatively, could the question be misphrased? Maybe "who won the Oscar for Wolf of Wall Street"? But the film didn't win any Oscars. Wait, maybe the user is asking about the Oscar winners related to The Wolf of Wall Street's categories. For example, who won Best Actor that year (instead of DiCaprio). Let's check.

The 2014 Oscars (for 2013 films):

Best Picture: 12 Years a Slave

Best Actor: Matthew McConaughey for Dallas Buyers Club (DiCaprio was nominated but didn't win)

Best Supporting Actor: Jared Leto for Dallas Buyers Club

Best Adapted Screenplay: The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated, but won by 12 Years a Slave (John Ridley)

So, if the user is asking who won the Oscar (Best Picture) over The Wolf of Wall Street, the answer would be 12 Years a Slave. But the question is phrased as "who won the oscar over wolf of wall street", which is a bit ambiguous. Maybe they meant which film won Best Picture instead of Wolf of Wall Street.

So, the task is to find the Best Picture winner for the year The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated.

First, I need to find the year The Wolf of Wall Street was eligible. The film was released in 2013, so the Oscars would be in 2014 (for 2013 films). So we need to find the Best Picture winner for the 2014 Academy Awards.

In Wikidata, The Wolf of Wall Street is Q134793. We need to find the Best Picture winner of the same year.

Steps:

1. Find the year of the Academy Awards for which The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated. The film's release year is 2013, so the ceremony is in 2014. But in Wikidata, the award ceremonies might have properties linking the film to the award.

2. Find the Best Picture winner for that year.

First, let's get the Oscar nominations for The Wolf of Wall Street.

Check if the film has a property like "nominee for Academy Award" or similar.

In Wikidata, The Wolf of Wall Street (Q134793) has awards:

- Academy Award for Best Actor (nominee: Leonardo DiCaprio)
- Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay (nominee: Terence Winter)
- Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor (nominee: Jonah Hill)

So, the film itself was not nominated for Best Picture, which complicates things. Wait, but the user says "won the Oscar over wolf of wall street". If the film wasn't nominated for Best Picture, then maybe the question is about another category? But the user isn't specific. Alternatively, maybe there's a misunderstanding here.

Wait, checking IMDb: The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated for five Oscars, including Best Picture? Wait no, according to IMDb, it was nominated for Best Picture? Wait, let me confirm.

Wait, according to the 86th Academy Awards (2014), Best Picture nominees were:

- American Hustle
- Captain Phillips
- Dallas Buyers Club
- Gravity
- Her
- Nebraska
- Philomena
- 12 Years a Slave
- The Wolf of Wall Street

Wait, yes, The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated for Best Picture. So 12 Years a Slave won Best Picture that year.

Therefore, the answer is "12 Years a Slave won the Best Picture Oscar over The Wolf of Wall Street".

So the user is asking who won the Oscar (Best Picture) for the year when The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated.

Thus, the task is to find the Best Picture winner of the same year that The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated (and lost).

So steps for SPARQL:

1. Find the film entity for "Wolf of Wall Street" (Q134793)
2. Find the award it was nominated for (Academy Award for Best Picture)
3. Check which film won that award (12 Years a Slave)

But in Wikidata, how to get the year?

2. Find the ceremony (86th Academy Awards Q722872)
3. Find the film that has P166 (award received) Q37194, with qualifier P179 (awarded at event) Q722872

But in SPARQL, the qualifier would be in a statement node.

In Wikidata's SPARQL, the structure for qualifiers uses the PS (PseudoStatement) and PQ (PseudoQualifier) properties.

So the query would look for:

under "award received":

- But also, the nominees are not directly listed on Q722872. Instead, the movie entities (like Q134793) might be linked as nominees.

- Academy Award for Best Picture is Q37194.

- 86th Academy Awards is Q722872.

So, find the ceremony where The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated for Best Picture.

Query to find the ceremony:

SELECT ?ceremony WHERE {
  wd:Q134793 wdt:P1411 wd:Q37194 .
  wd:Q134793 wdt:P1344 ?ceremony .  # P1344 is 'award ceremony', but not sure.
}

But how to link nomination to ceremony.

Assuming that the nomination (P1411) has a qualifier linking to the ceremony.

In SPARQL, to find the ceremony for the nomination:

SELECT ?ceremony WHERE {
  wd:Q134793 p:P1411 ?nomination .
  ?nomination ps:P1411 wd:Q37194 .
  ?nomination pq:P179 ?ceremony .  # P179 is "awarded at event", commonly used as qualifier for award ceremonies
}

If that works, then ?ceremony would be Q722872.

Once we have the ceremony, we can find the winner:

SELECT ?winner WHERE {
  ?winner wdt:P166 wd:Q37194 .  # ?winner received award Best Picture

}

}

Alternatively, check if the ceremony Q722872 has a property that points to the winner.

But in Wikidata model, it's more likely that the winner has a backward link to the ceremony.

2. Get the winner for that award at that ceremony.

Let's try drafting the SPARQL.

First, verify if The Wolf of Wall Street's Best Picture nomination links to the ceremony.

Query:

SELECT ?ceremony WHERE {
  wd:Q134793 p:P1411 ?nomination .
  ?nomination ps:P1411 wd:Q37194 .
  ?nomination pq:P179 ?ceremony .
}

But does this triple exist in Wikidata?

Assuming P179 is the correct qualifier (it's widely used for "awarded at event"), and if the nomination statement has that qualifier, then this would return the ceremony.

If that's not present, maybe the ceremony is linked through another property.

Alternatively, the nomination may not have the ceremony qualifier, but knowing it's the 86th Academy Awards, we can look for the ceremony by year.

Alternative approach:

The Wolf of Wall Street was released in 2013. The ceremony is in 2014. The Oscar for films of year Y takes place in year Y+1.

So, get the publication year of the film, then find the award ceremony in the following year.

First, get the release year of The Wolf of Wall Street.

Query:

SELECT ?year WHERE {
  wd:Q134793 wdt:P577 ?date .
  BIND(YEAR(?date) AS ?year)
}

This might return 2013.

Then, knowing that the ceremony was in 2014, find the Best Picture award winner for the 2014 ceremony (which is for 2013 films).

But how to link ceremony year to the award.

Alternative query:

Find all movies that won Academy Award for Best Picture (Q37194), and find which one has a ceremony in the year after The Wolf of Wall Street's release.

But this is getting convoluted.

Given time constraints, perhaps the most straightforward way is:

Assume that the user is asking for the Best Picture winner the year The Wolf of Wall Street was nominated (2013 films), which is 12 Years a Slave.

Construct a query that gets the winner of Q37194 (Best Picture) for the ceremony related to 2013 films.

Another angle: look up which film won Best Picture in the same year that The Wolf of Wall Street was eligible. Since the film was released in 2013, the eligible year is 2013.

The query could be:

SELECT ?winner ?winnerLabel WHERE {
  wd:Q134793 wdt:P577 ?releaseDate .
  BIND(YEAR(?releaseDate) AS ?filmYear)
  ?awardCeremony wdt:P808 ?filmYear .  # P808 is "edition", but not sure
  # Not sure how to connect ceremony year
}

This might not work.

Alternative idea: Academy Award for Best Picture (Q37194) has winners each year. Each winner has a qualifier "point in time" (P585) indicating the year of the award.

So:

SELECT ?winner ?winnerLabel WHERE {
  ?winner wdt:P166 wd:Q37194 .
  ?winner p:P166/ps:P166 wd:Q37194 .
  ?winner p:P166/pq:P585 ?awardDate .
